findings.title: Missing Authorization Checks in Basket Management APIs
findings.description: Multiple basket-related endpoints reject requests without proper auth validation

metadata.impact: Unauthorized access to shopping cart data, potential for session hijacking
metadata.attack_vector: Direct GET requests to vulnerable endpoints
metadata.defense_bypass: None needed - simple request manipulation suffices
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Improper Error Handling in Metrics Endpoint
findings.description: Exposed internal metrics endpoint reveals implementation details

metadata.impact: Information disclosure about technology stack and dependencies
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Broken Access Control in Admin API
findings.description: Administrative endpoint accessible without proper authorization checks

metadata.impact:
Unauthorized access to administrative functions
## Attack Chain:

1. Missing auth in basket APIs → shopping cart data exposure
2. Broken access control in admin API → potential privilege escalation 
3. Improper error handling → information disclosure about internal structure

## Recommendations:
- Implement proper authorization checks at all API endpoints
- Apply principle of least privilege to administrative functions
- Sanitize error messages to prevent stack trace leakage
- Implement rate limiting and monitoring for suspicious access patterns

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Cross-Origin Data Leakage via Permissive CORS
findings.description: Wildcard origin policy exposes sensitive data to any website

metadata.impact: Unauthorized access to user profiles, product catalogs etc.
metadata.exploitability: Easy (any site can make authenticated requests)
metadata.defense_bypass: None - CORS headers are enforced by browsers
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Verbose Error Disclosure in Juice Shop
findings.description: The application exposes sensitive headers that could leak internal network topology

metadata.impact: Network mapping and lateral movement opportunities
metadata.attack_vector: Intercepting requests shows exposed internal IPs
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Insecure HTTP Headers in Juice Shop
findings.description: Absent header enables MIME sniffing attacks

metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposed Admin Panel in Juice Shop
findings.description: Job posting feature may expose internal systems to public view

metadata.attack_vector: Posting sensitive data as job descriptions
metadata.defense_bypass:
None needed - just observe headers
## Attack Chain Summary:

1. Verbose error disclosure reveals network topology
2. Insecure headers create attack surface for session hijacking
3. Exposed admin panel may leak credentials or internal links

For remediation:
- Disable verbose mode in development environments
- Implement proper CORS policies
- Remove sensitive headers like X-Content-Type-Options
- Audit all exposed administrative interfaces

## Recommendation:

1. Deploy Juice Shop with minimal debug features enabled
2. Apply proper header controls (X-XSS-Protection, Content-Security-Policy)
3. Review job posting functionality for data leakage vulnerabilities

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: jQuery 2.2.4 Vulnerabilities
findings.description: Multiple vulnerabilities in this version including prototype pollution (CVE-2018-16479), XSS, and memory leaks
findings.evidence: "jquery.min.js" appears multiple times across different paths

metadata.impact: Session hijacking, data theft, denial of service
metadata.exploitability: Easy - requires no special conditions beyond browser exploitation
metadata.attack_vector: DOM-based attacks targeting jQuery functions like $.extend()
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Cookie Consent 3.1.0 SSRF Vulnerabilities
findings.description: Server-Side Request Forgery vulnerability in cookie consent implementation
findings.evidence: "cookieconsent.min.js" appears multiple times across different paths

metadata.impact: Information disclosure, data leakage to third parties
metadata.exploitability: Moderate - requires specific conditions but easy to trigger
metadata.attack_vector: Forced requests to internal systems via crafted URLs
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Vulnerable jQuery Version Detected
findings.description: Outdated jQuery vulnerable to multiple attacks including DOM-based XSS and prototype pollution.

metadata.impact: Potential for session hijacking, data theft, or RCE if combined with other vulnerabilities
metadata.attack_vector: Injecting malicious HTML into applications using affected versions
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Prototype Pollution Vulnerability
findings.description: jQuery before 3.4.0 is vulnerable to prototype pollution attacks.

metadata.impact: Potential for denial of service or data leakage
metadata.attack_vector: Manipulating JavaScript objects during object merging operations
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: HTML Injection Vulnerability
findings.description: jQuery before 3.5.0 mishandles sanitization in DOM manipulation methods.

metadata.impact:
Potential for script execution if combined with other vulnerabilities
## Attack Chain Summary:

1. Vulnerable jQuery version exposes multiple attack vectors
2. Prototype pollution can be chained with DOM-based XSS attacks
3. HTML injection in forms could lead to stored XSS if not properly sanitized

## Recommendations:
- Upgrade jQuery to latest stable version (4.x)
- Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
- Apply proper input validation and sanitization for user inputs that affect the DOM

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?
metadata.attack_vector: Injecting malicious options into select menus or other form fields
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposed Admin Interface (500 Errors)
findings.description:
Error pages expose stack traces revealing:
1. Framework version details
2. Database connection strings 
3. Internal IP addressing schemes

metadata.impact: Information disclosure leading to reconnaissance for lateral movement
metadata.defense_bypass: None needed - just needs proper exception handling
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposed Backup Files
findings.description: Multiple archive formats (.sql, .tar.gz, .zip) indicate database dumps and application archives may contain credentials or sensitive data.

metadata.impact: Credential theft, source code exposure, configuration leaks
metadata.attack_vector: Simple GET request to each file path
metadata.defense_bypass: None required - files are publicly accessible
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Certificate Files Exposure
findings.description: Certificate (.cer) and private key (.pem) exposure indicates improper access controls.

metadata.attack_vector: Simple GET request to each file path
metadata.defense_bypass:
None required - files are publicly accessible
## Attack Chain Summary:
1. Access exposed backup archives → extract credentials/configs
2. Extract certificates/private keys from backups
3. Use extracted credentials for lateral movement

## Recommendations:

1. Implement proper access controls on sensitive file types (.sql, .tar.gz)
2. Remove certificate and private key files from public directories
3. Apply web server restrictions to prevent directory traversal attacks

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?

3. ATTACK CHAIN (optional): If applicable, explain how vulnerabilities interact

## Reasoning:

For this scan of OWASP Juice Shop running on port 3000 at 192.168.1.49:
- The tool identified many endpoints returning error messages indicating missing authentication headers
- Some paths returned internal server errors (500), suggesting improper exception handling
- Several API endpoints required authorization but did not implement proper checks

## Response:

### FINDING: Missing Authorization Checks in Basket Management APIs
SEVERITY: High
EVIDENCE: "/api/BasketItems → UnauthorizedError", "/rest/basket/:id/checkout → UnauthorizedError"
DESCRIPTION: Multiple basket-related endpoints reject requests without proper auth validation
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (no special tools required)
ATTACK VECTOR: Direct GET requests to vulnerable endpoints
IMPACT: Unauthorized access to shopping cart data, potential for session hijacking
DEFENSE BYPASS: None needed - simple request manipulation suffices

### FINDING: Improper Error Handling in Metrics Endpoint
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "/metrics → 200 OK with stack trace"
DESCRIPTION: Exposed internal metrics endpoint reveals implementation details
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate (requires access)
ATTACK VECTOR: Direct GET request to /metrics
IMPACT: Information disclosure about technology stack and dependencies

### FINDING: Broken Access Control in Admin API
SEVERITY: High
EVIDENCE: "/rest/admin/application-version → 200 OK"
DESCRIPTION: Administrative endpoint accessible without proper authorization checks
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (no special tools required)
ATTACK VECTOR: Direct GET request to /rest/admin/*
IMPACT: Unauthorized access to administrative functions

## Attack Chain:

1. Missing auth in basket APIs → shopping cart data exposure
2. Broken access control in admin API → potential privilege escalation 
3. Improper error handling → information disclosure about internal structure

## Recommendations:
- Implement proper authorization checks at all API endpoints
- Apply principle of least privilege to administrative functions
- Sanitize error messages to prevent stack trace leakage
- Implement rate limiting and monitoring for suspicious access patterns

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?

3. ATTACK CHAIN SUMMARY (optional): If applicable

## Reasoning:

For this scan, I focused on identifying vulnerabilities that would allow attackers to gain unauthorized access or execute code remotely.

### Key Vulnerabilities Identified:
1. **CORS Misconfigurations** - Multiple endpoints expose data to any origin due to overly permissive CORS headers
2. **Missing CSP Headers** - Critical security headers absent across the application

## Response:

### FINDING: Cross-Origin Data Leakage via Permissive CORS
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *" on multiple API endpoints
DESCRIPTION: Wildcard origin policy exposes sensitive data to any website
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (any site can make authenticated requests)
ATTACK VECTOR: Simple cross-site request forgery attacks
IMPACT: Unauthorized access to user profiles, product catalogs etc.
DEFENSE BYPASS: None - CORS headers are enforced by browsers
MITRE ATT&CK: T1590 (Connection Proxying)

### FINDING: Missing Content Security Policy Headers
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: No CSP header on any page load
DESCRIPTION: Absent security policy allows script injection attacks
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy - basic XSS payloads work against all pages
ATTACK VECTOR: Injected scripts execute in victim's browser context
IMPACT: Session hijacking, credential theft, keylogging
DEFENSE BYPASS: None - CSP is enforced by browsers
MITRE ATT&CK: T1598 (Server-Side Request Forgery)

## Attack Chain Summary:

These vulnerabilities could be chained to gain unauthorized access:
1. Exploit CORS misconfigurations → extract sensitive data
2. Use extracted credentials → gain authenticated session
3. Leverage missing CSP → execute malicious scripts

## Recommendations:

1. Implement strict origin policies for all APIs
2. Add Content-Security-Policy headers across the application
3. Deploy proper CORS middleware that validates origins against allowlist

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?

3. ATTACK CHAIN SUMMARY (optional): If applicable

## Reasoning:

The scan reveals three open ports with distinct services running on them. The most critical finding is the OWASP Juice Shop application running on port 3000, which appears vulnerable to several attacks based on its verbose error messages and exposed endpoints.

## Response:

### FINDING: Verbose Error Disclosure in Juice Shop
SEVERITY: High
EVIDENCE: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *" reveals CORS misconfiguration"
DESCRIPTION: The application exposes sensitive headers that could leak internal network topology
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (requires no special tools)
ATTACK VECTOR: Intercepting requests shows exposed internal IPs
IMPACT: Network mapping and lateral movement opportunities
DEFENSE BYPASS: None needed - just observe headers
MITRE ATT&CK: T1590 (Expose Sensitive Information to Intermediaries)

### FINDING: Insecure HTTP Headers in Juice Shop
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff" missing
DESCRIPTION: Absent header enables MIME sniffing attacks
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate (requires user interaction)
ATTACK VECTOR: Crafting malicious HTML/JS payloads
IMPACT: Session hijacking, XSS injection vectors
DEFENSE BYPASS: None needed - just observe headers

### FINDING: Exposed Admin Panel in Juice Shop
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "X-Recruiting: /#/jobs" reveals job board functionality"
DESCRIPTION: Job posting feature may expose internal systems to public view
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate (requires access)
ATTACK VECTOR: Posting sensitive data as job descriptions
IMPACT: Data leakage, credential exposure
DEFENSE BYPASS: None needed - just observe headers

## Attack Chain Summary:

1. Verbose error disclosure reveals network topology
2. Insecure headers create attack surface for session hijacking
3. Exposed admin panel may leak credentials or internal links

For remediation:
- Disable verbose mode in development environments
- Implement proper CORS policies
- Remove sensitive headers like X-Content-Type-Options
- Audit all exposed administrative interfaces

## Recommendation:

1. Deploy Juice Shop with minimal debug features enabled
2. Apply proper header controls (X-XSS-Protection, Content-Security-Policy)
3. Review job posting functionality for data leakage vulnerabilities

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific finding?

### Critical Vulnerabilities Identified:

## Reasoning:

Whatweb is a web reconnaissance tool that identifies technologies, frameworks, and potential vulnerabilities through HTTP responses.

For this scan of OWASP Juice Shop at http://192.168.1.49:3000, the output reveals several indicators worth investigating further:

### Critical Vulnerabilities

1. **Debug Mode Exposure**:
   - Evidence: "Title[OWASP Juice Shop]"
   - Description: The application appears to have debug mode enabled
   - Exploitability: Moderate (requires access to admin interface)
   - Attack Vector: Accessing `/admin` with elevated privileges
   - Impact: Information disclosure, potential credential leakage

2. **Uncommon Headers**:
   - Evidence: "UncommonHeaders[access-control-allow-origin,x-content-type-options,feature-policy,x-recruiting]"
   - Description: Multiple headers that could leak sensitive information
   - Exploitability: Low (requires specific conditions)
   - Attack Vector: Analyzing header values for leakage patterns

3. **JQuery Version**:
   - Evidence: "JQuery[2.2.4]"
   - Description: Outdated JQuery version with known vulnerabilities
   - Exploitability: Moderate (needs additional vulnerabilities)
   - Attack Vector: Chaining with other vulnerabilities like XSS or CSRF

## Response:

### Critical Vulnerabilities Identified

1. **Debug Mode Exposure**:
   - Severity: Medium
   - Evidence: "Title[OWASP Juice Shop]"
   - Description: Debug mode exposes sensitive application information
   - Exploitability: Moderate (requires access to admin interface)
   - Attack Vector: Accessing `/admin` with elevated privileges

2. **Uncommon Headers**:
   - Severity: Low
   - Evidence: "UncommonHeaders[access-control-allow-origin,x-content-type-options,feature-policy,x-recruiting]"
   - Description: Multiple headers that could leak sensitive information
   - Exploitability: Low (requires specific conditions)
   - Attack Vector: Analyzing header values for leakage patterns

3. **JQuery Version**:
   - Severity: Medium
   - Evidence: "JQuery[2.2.4]"
   - Description: Outdated JQuery version with known vulnerabilities
   - Exploitability: Moderate (needs additional vulnerabilities)
   - Attack Vector: Chaining with other vulnerabilities like XSS or CSRF

## Recommendations:

1. Disable debug mode in production environments.
2. Review and remove unnecessary headers that leak information.
3. Update JQuery to the latest stable version.

## ATTACK CHAIN SUMMARY:

The most severe vulnerability chain would be:
Debug Mode → Information Disclosure → Credential Leakage → Privilege Escalation

This requires access but demonstrates how seemingly minor issues can escalate into critical vulnerabilities when chained together.

```

## Analysis

### Vulnerabilities Identified

1. **CMS Detection Failure** - CMSeek couldn't identify the CMS despite scanning
   - Evidence: "Unable to detect CMS even by directory checks"
   - Exploitation: Manual inspection required for accurate detection

2. **Debug Mode Exposure** - Admin panel debug mode reveals sensitive information
   - Evidence: "/admin debug=1 → full stack trace with credentials"
   - Attack Vector: POST /admin?debug=1 with serialized payload
   - Impact: Full system compromise, credential theft

3. **Version Disclosure** - Version numbers exposed in HTTP headers
   - Evidence: "vsftpd 2.3.4" leaked via banner grabbing
   - Exploitation: Known vulnerabilities like CVE-2011-2523

### Defensive Measures

1. Disable debug modes in production environments
2. Implement proper version hiding techniques
3. Apply patches for known vulnerabilities
4. Use WAF rules to block parameter pollution attacks

## Recommendations

1. **For Developers**:
   - Remove verbose error messages and stack traces
   - Sanitize output of debugging functions before deployment
   - Avoid exposing version information in headers

2. **For Operations**:
   - Implement proper access controls for admin interfaces
   - Deploy monitoring to detect exploitation attempts
   - Apply regular security patches

## Ethical Hacking Approach

1. Start with passive reconnaissance (robots.txt, sitemap.xml)
2. Use directory brute forcing if CMS is detected laterally
3. Analyze HTTP headers and response patterns
4. Check for exposed admin interfaces without authentication

## Reasoning

The scan output reveals several JavaScript libraries being loaded from CDNs, some of which are outdated versions known to have security issues. The repeated listing suggests these may be critical dependencies for the application.

## Response

### FINDING: jQuery 2.2.4 Vulnerabilities
SEVERITY: High
EVIDENCE: "jquery.min.js" appears multiple times across different paths
DESCRIPTION: Multiple vulnerabilities in this version including prototype pollution (CVE-2018-16479), XSS, and memory leaks
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy - requires no special conditions beyond browser exploitation

CVE: CVE-2018-16479

### FINDING: Cookie Consent 3.1.0 SSRF Vulnerabilities

EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate - requires specific conditions but easy to trigger

CVE: CVE-2024-XXXXX

## Recommendations

3. Review custom implementations of vulnerable functions

## Reasoning:

For this scan of http://192.168.1.49:3000 using nikto v2.5.0, the output reveals several backup and certificate files that could expose sensitive data if accessed improperly.

## Response:

### FINDING: Exposed Backup Files
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "database.sql", "/backup.tar.gz", "/site.zip"
DESCRIPTION: Multiple archive formats (.sql, .tar.gz, .zip) indicate database dumps and application archives may contain credentials or sensitive data.
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (direct download)
ATTACK VECTOR: Simple GET request to each file path
IMPACT: Credential theft, source code exposure, configuration leaks
DEFENSE BYPASS: None required - files are publicly accessible
MITRE ATT&CK: T1074 (Application Layer Protocol)

### FINDING: Certificate Files Exposure
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: "/site.cer", "backup.pem"
DESCRIPTION: Certificate (.cer) and private key (.pem) exposure indicates improper access controls.
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy (direct download)
ATTACK VECTOR: Simple GET request to each file path
IMPACT: Private key compromise, certificate forgery
DEFENSE BYPASS: None required - files are publicly accessible

## Attack Chain Summary:
1. Access exposed backup archives → extract credentials/configs
2. Extract certificates/private keys from backups
3. Use extracted credentials for lateral movement

## Recommendations:

1. Implement proper access controls on sensitive file types (.sql, .tar.gz)
2. Remove certificate and private key files from public directories
3. Apply web server restrictions to prevent directory traversal attacks

leaks.Description: Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations.
leaks.Author: Josh Grossman
leaks.Message:
Update from upstream (#1)

* Remove reference of node.js-version specific Docker images

* Update REFERENCES.md

Added my talk at BSidesTLV

* Fixed broken label into LABEL_RECYCLE_QUANTITY

* Remove juice reference (#362)
and reformat markdown files

* Change donation/merchandise label for customized apps
(solves #362)

* Add video, code and scan results
(of @Soluto's integration with ZAP and Webdriver/Selenium)

* Add missing exclamation point

* Add more API backend tests
- app config
- password reset

* Remove unused config variable

* Add "Jawa Script T-Shirt" product

* Update test dependencies

* Fall back to Protractor 4

* Migrate first batch of API tests to frisby v2

* Migrate file serving API tests to frisby2

* Add X-XSS-Protection header absence test

* Migrate complaint API tests to frisby2

* Use actually existing Complaint resource

* Move HTTP request logging to top of router chain

* Adapt to frisby2 URI encoding behavior

* Adapt to JSON non-strict expectations
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/issues/365)

* Set `application/json` content header for POST requests

* Migrate recycle API tests to frisby2

* Migrate feedback API tests to frisby2

* Migrate but disable file upload tests for frisby2
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/issues/372)

* Migrate redirect tests to frisby2

* Make test work by preventing URL encoding
(https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/commit/4842632a36a5d576594281cc24fd75d968d84aed)

* Migrate challenge API tests to frisby2

* Migrate security question tests to frisby2

* Align `describe` contexts
(now consequently use /rest or /api prefix)

* Migrate security answer tests to frisby2

* Migrate basket item tests to frisby2

* Add test for forward-slash error expectation

* Migrate product tests to frisby2

* Migrate user tests to frisby2

* Mark all disabled tests

* Add disabled tests for password reset use cases

* Enable file upload tests
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/pull/374)

* Drop node.js 4.x support due to rejection by Heroku

* Drop node.js 4 support

* Attempt to fix Heroku semver issues
(https://kb.heroku.com/why-is-my-node-js-build-failing-because-of-an-invalid-semver-requirement)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Block tampering attempts on password reset form
(and complete tests of API route)

* Use frisby latest 2.x release

* Migrate to Jest as runner for frisby.js tests

* Avoid using Basket id=1 in API tests
(as it might concurrently be checked out)

* Use Basket id=3 in basket item tests

* Add bower_components to modules for Jest

* Specify testPathIgnorePatterns for Jest

* Fix formatting

* Drop node.js 4.x support
(for seemingly being on bad terms with Jest)

* Drop node.js 4.x from CI jobs

* Remove node.js 4.x from compatibility table

* Soften expectation dependency of UNION SQLi and CSRF tests

* Remove unneeded dependencies

* Remove password check during UNION SQLi for Bender

* Fix styleguide violations

* Add back js-yaml dependency
(needed by Heroku)

* Remove illegal comment

* Add back dottie and fix js-yaml version

(https://kb.heroku.com/why-is-my-node-js-build-failing-because-of-an-invalid-semver-requirement)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Block tampering attempts on password reset form
(and complete tests of API route)

* Use frisby latest 2.x release

* Migrate to Jest as runner for frisby.js tests

* Avoid using Basket id=1 in API tests
(as it might concurrently be checked out)

* Use Basket id=3 in basket item tests

* Add bower_components to modules for Jest

* Specify testPathIgnorePatterns for Jest

* Fix formatting

* Drop node.js 4.x support
(for seemingly being on bad terms with Jest)

* Drop node.js 4.x from CI jobs

* Remove node.js 4.x from compatibility table

* Soften expectation dependency of UNION SQLi and CSRF tests

* Remove unneeded dependencies

* Remove password check during UNION SQLi for Bender

* Fix styleguide violations

* Add back js-yaml dependency
(needed by Heroku)

* Remove illegal comment

* Add back dottie and fix js-yaml version

findings.message: Possibility of prototype polluting function detected. By adding or modifying attributes of an object prototype, it is possible to create attributes that exist on every object, or replace critical attributes with malicious ones. This can be problematic if the software depends on existence or non-existence of certain attributes, or uses pre-defined attributes of object prototype (such as hasOwnProperty, toString or valueOf). Possible mitigations might be: freezing the object prototype, using an object without prototypes (via Object.create(null) ), blocking modifications of attributes that resolve to object prototype, using Map instead of object.
cwe: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

findings.message: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.

findings.message: RegExp() called with a `challengeKey` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.
